summary: Joel Spolsky responds to a Groove Networks designer who objected to being called an 'Architecture Astronaut.' While acknowledging Groove's elegant peer-to-peer architecture that improves on Lotus Notes by eliminating server bottlenecks, Spolsky argues that architecture alone doesn't sell products — killer apps and compelling features do. He draws parallels to Notes, which succeeded as an email client despite being sold as a groupware platform, and to NeXT and Be, which had great architectures but failed commercially. His central argument is that Groove needs to demonstrate specific, compelling use cases rather than relying on 'third parties will build things' as a strategy.
key_points: Groove's peer-to-peer architecture is essentially 'Notes done right' by eliminating the server bottleneck that plagued large Notes installations
Lotus Notes was the most misunderstood product in history — sold as groupware but bought and used as email, which ironically didn't work well enough
Architecture doesn't sell products; features and killer apps do, as demonstrated by the commercial failures of NeXT and Be despite superior architectures
Relying on third parties to build compelling apps on your platform is a weak strategy unless you can articulate what those killer apps will be
Repeatedly hearing the same contrived example use case (like GPS coffee shop coupons) is a warning sign that a platform lacks genuine real-world demand
money_quotes: It's the applets that are going to sell Groove, not the architecture.
You can build the best dang computer in the world with a killer architecture, but if it doesn't run Microsoft Excel, nobody wants one.
When you say 'it's a platform, third parties will build things,' you have to explain what KIND of things and why THEY will be killer apps that sell the architecture.
Notes was the most misunderstood product in history.
It was being sold as a groupware platform, but in reality, it was being bought (and used) as an email program.
It amuses me, because it solves the one problem that coffee shops DON'T have, namely, advertising to people who are standing right in front of the store!
If you want it to be The Next Great Thing it has to be more than architecture, it has to enable things that people really need.
key_insight: Great architecture is necessary but insufficient for product success — what matters is whether the platform enables specific, compelling features that solve problems people actually have.
